name: GPT-3.5 Augmentation of Small Student Text Response Sets
population_or_context: French opinion responses and English sixth-grade rainwater-runoff responses.
sample_size: French dataset: 163 responses across four labels; runoff dataset: 95 sixth-grade responses coded across six binary concepts.
methods: GPT-3.5 completions paraphrased actual student responses at multiple temperature settings; augmented data trained multilingual BERT classifiers.
education_context: Student text responses in two languages, including sixth-grade environmental science/rainwater-runoff concepts and a French opinion task.
response_origin: Hybrid: original student responses plus GPT-3.5-generated artificial student responses.
rubric_label_space: One four-label opinion classification task and six binary concept-presence classifications.
label_source: Original datasets provided concept or opinion labels; generated responses were paraphrases of labeled student responses.
generation_method: Prompt-based paraphrase generation from actual student responses at temperature values 0.1, 0.5, and 0.9.
prompt_strategy: The prompt used 'paraphrase this sentence' plus an actual student response.
evaluation_method: Multilingual BERT classifiers were fine-tuned and evaluated with micro-F1 against unaugmented, majority-class, and self-augmentation baselines.
scorer_use: Training augmentation for automated evaluation/classification of student text responses.
real_response_comparator: Yes. Original held-out real responses were used for testing; self-augmentation and unaugmented BERT baselines were compared.
sparsity_or_imbalance_motivation: The paper frames scarcity of student-generated data and imbalanced small educational datasets as the motivation.
synthetic_data_research_role: Generated additional training examples to supplement small response sets and test whether synthetic examples beat simple replication.
reported_metrics: Augmented models improved over unaugmented BERT in all seven concepts; GPT-3.5 generation produced the maximum performance in four of seven cases.
limitations: Very heavily imbalanced low-entropy datasets could still be better served by majority-label baselines; performance degraded after high augmentation levels.
